So today we're going to talk not about your product but about your prospect. And as I think I mentioned yesterday, probably the biggest room for improvement in copy is not so much to write better about the product but to write better about the prospect. Not to understand the product better but to understand the prospect better. What goes on inside his or her head. How they're really thinking. What they really want. You know and not just superficially what they want. They want to do a better job or they want to make more money. But when they lie awake at night what keeps them awake? You know what are the things they secret. The secret things that they dream about. The secret things that they fear that keep them up at night. So I wanted to go over a few ways of thinking about the prospect. A few questions to ask yourself. And we have something called the six W's. Has anyone ever heard of that? It's kind of a journalism thing for writing for newspapers. Who, what, when, where, how and why. So if you ask yourself those questions about the prospect you can get a really deep and more thorough understanding. So the first W is who. So not just who are they. You know who are they? Who do they want to be? What role do they want to play in life? Do they want to be a better father? Do they want to be a better mother? Do they want to be admired by people? So who is it that they, not just who they are right now but who they want to be. Who they see themselves as being. Maybe someone sees themselves as a really good father. A really good, you know of course purchasing manager probably sees themselves as a really good purchasing manager. How else do they see themselves? Do they see themselves as the sort of person that other people come to with questions that other people come to for advice? Do they want to see themselves in that way? Is that something we can talk about in the copy? So who is the first one and then what? Starting with what, what do they read? What do they look at? Do they read certain newspapers and not others? In America we have newspapers that are very conservative. We have TV stations like Fox News that are very conservative. We have other news stations like National Public Radio, the broadcasting, Radio Public Government funded broadcasting that's very liberal and what we call left wing. So which ones do your clients, your prospects read? Which ones do they look at? What are they, and then what in terms of what do they love? What do they hate? What are the things in their life that they feel strongly about? What do they love? What do they hate? What do they fear? What do they worry about? What are they proud of? What's their physical and emotional pain? All these what questions that you need to ask yourself about the prospect. And the third W is when? When are they looking at? When are they reading your promotion? What's going on in their lives when they're doing that? What are the times that are important to them? If it's a mother, birthdays are important. If it's someone working in an office, if it's a tax accountant, April 15th is important. What dates are important to your prospect? And how can you use them in promotion like someone did yesterday, which is what reminded me of it where they said April 15th something's going to happen or important things or the German elections are an important date for everyone. I think you said there were European elections, a lot of elections going on under a certain date. So really think about in terms of timing, you know, do people buy your product? Do people tend to order your product at a certain time of year? Do they order it when an event happens in their life? Some products people order when they get married, some products people order. If it's a product for people, how to get a job, maybe it's after they just lost their job or something like that. So what's the timing for your product? Where? As I said, where are they, where are they when they see your promotion? Where do they live? Where do they hang out? Do they go to certain sites and not others? Are they in certain forums and not others? Are they, do they tend to live in cities? Do they tend to live in suburbs? All of these are kind of where questions. Who, what, when, where, why? Very important, why do they do things? What motivates them? Why do they, of course, why do they buy your product? But what motivates them in general? You know, what do they live for? What do they care about? Do they, you know, like the people that subscribe to Save Money? There, why is to be safe? They care about being safe. People that, that, surprise to an options related, their, their why is not to be safe so much. Their why is to make money. They're willing to take some chances. And then the last one, it doesn't begin with the W, but it's got the W at the end. Which is how? How do they do things? How do they look at the world? What's their world view? Do they value the opinions of others? Do they form their own world view? Do they view the world as a safe place, as a dangerous place? How do they go about making purchasing decisions? How do they go about buying things? How do they go about specifically buying your product, your publication? How do you find out these things? Obviously, you can ask your prospects. You can talk to them. You can call them up. You can have meetings. You can send surveys. Any of you ever use something called Survey Monkey? I assume they have that here in the United States, in Germany. Yeah. I mean, that's a great way to find out. But you can only get but so much depth asking questions in text. There's a different depth that you get when you have an interchange. So as much as possible, you really want to be talking face to face to your clients, getting them in front of you, taking them to lunch, going to where they work. Next best thing is social media, forums, seeing how they communicate with each other on social media. What kind of words do they use? That's one thing that goes up here in terms of what. What type of words do they use? What's their vocabulary? How do they talk about their pain? How do they talk about investing? How do they talk about doing their job? Do they call themselves, in the United States, we have a thing, do they call themselves sufferers or not? Do they call themselves arthritis sufferers? Or is that not a word they would use about themselves? They don't like to be seen as suffering. Because if you talk about them in a way they talk about themselves, they'll feel that you're one of them. They'll feel like you're a friend. They'll be more likely to buy from you. Amazon is a great place to probably list these. So you've got social media and you've got Amazon. Now there's two things about Amazon that can help you. One is the book titles, the subtitles. There's a lot of great stuff in there about how people look at their problem, about what resonates with them, what appeals to them, the best selling book in a certain category tells you a whole lot about what's going on. The description of the books, those descriptions are really well written. The description on Amazon or the description on a book jacket in a bookstore. Bookstore is kind of like same thing as Amazon, right? It's really a visit to a bookstore. You look at the flap, what does it say about the book, what does it say on the back? And then on Amazon, the reviews. Because the reviews are people talking about their situation, about their pain, about their needs and their wants. A book on Amazon about arthritis and it says, oh, I'm looking for a product that tells me what's safe. And this book didn't tell me what's safe. So you know that safe products, they're looking for safe things. There's reviews of supplements that give you a lot of insight into how people look at their diseases. And then of course, googling. A lot of times you can even literally Google something like, what is it like to have diabetes? And you'll find something on Quora or on some forum, which is someone writing about 10 things people that don't have diabetes that don't know about what it's like. 10 things people who are overweight wish that other people knew about being overweight. 10 things that makes us angry when we have arthritis that people do or say. If you know that, that's like gold for being able to talk about in a letter, for showing people that you understand, you understand their situation. And you can literally use things that people say, interviewing a lot of times I have and I know other people who have literally taken something a prospect has said and used that in the copy. Whether it's as a quote or just kind of working it into the copy. Because you know it's true. You know it resonates with them. You know it'll be powerful. One thing I wanted to mention going back to who, one of the who's is also who is their enemy. And we've talked about that a little bit here. Is the government their enemy when they're trying to be safe investors? For people who are, have health problems. A lot of times the drug companies are the enemies that make these drugs that don't help them get better, that have side effects that they grow addicted to. So who's the enemy for your group? Who's the enemy for purchasing agents, for human resources agents? Is it the employees? Is it the boss? Is it the government making all these regulations? They have to follow every day and that drives them crazy and makes their job so hard. If you show in your letter that their enemy is your enemy, it's a very powerful way to bond and to make them feel like you have their best interests at heart.
